narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 48.4% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt, which is high enough to flag concerns for admission. Your profile shows you ranked in the bottom decile for grades in both your first and third years of school—much lower than the typical student's middle-of-the-pack position around the fifth or sixth decile. On a brighter note, your LSAT score of 39 and undergraduate GPA of 3.40 both stand out as stronger than average, while your middle family income bracket, male gender, White racial background, and full-time student status align closely with the majority of applicants in the dataset. When the model weighed all this, your low Year 1 grade decile emerged as the single most influential factor, nudging the prediction toward expecting a bar exam struggle. Right behind it in importance was your similarly low Year 3 grade decile, followed closely by your middle family income, each contributing in a way that reinforced the risk of failure. Your LSAT score and undergraduate GPA came next in influence, still tilting slightly toward the failure prediction despite their above-average strength, with your racial background, gender, and full-time status having the least sway. Overall, these early academic rankings set you apart from typical students who tend to have steadier mid-range performance, pulling your prediction into risky territory even with your later achievements. It's clear the model saw your foundational grades as the biggest hurdles compared to peers. Looking ahead, you can't change past deciles, but focusing on building even stronger habits—like those that boosted your LSAT and GPA—could help in bar preparation or future academic showings. Consider targeted study strategies to mimic the consistency of higher-decile students, perhaps through practice exams or tutoring that addresses early weaknesses. Retaking the LSAT for an even higher score might amplify its positive signal if opportunities arise. With your solid recent metrics, dedicated bar prep could realistically shift your odds toward success on the first try. Keep pushing—you've already shown improvement potential.
